front.title: Cyborgs and Identity
front.subtitle: Exploring the blurred boundaries between biological life and synthetic enhancement.

back.text:
The term cyborg, a portmanteau of cybernetic organism, describes a being with both biological and artificial components. In science fiction and philosophy, the cyborg serves as a thought experiment for the future of human evolution. As we integrate pacemakers, neural implants, and advanced prosthetics into the human body, the distinction between man and machine becomes increasingly fluid. This technological convergence challenges traditional definitions of personhood and physical autonomy.

Central to the study of cyborgs is the question of identity: at what point does a technologically augmented human cease to be human? Science fiction often explores this through characters who struggle to reconcile their organic memories with their mechanical capabilities. In a practical sense, modern brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) are already turning these fictional concepts into reality, allowing individuals to control external devices with their thoughts and raising new questions about where the self ends and the software begins.
